gh-w10-f1.answer:
EmployeeAPI/ — production-quality Employee Management REST API with DTOs, pagination, validation, Swagger docs, and standardized error responses.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — web, data-jpa, mysql, validation, springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui
• src/main/java/com/academy/employee/
  - EmployeeApiApplication.java
  - model/Employee.java (@Entity)
  - dto/EmployeeRequest.java, EmployeeResponse.java
  - mapper/EmployeeMapper.java — entity ↔ DTO conversion
  - repository/EmployeeRepository.java
  - service/EmployeeService.java
  - controller/EmployeeController.java (@RequestMapping("/api/v1/employees"))
  - exception/GlobalExceptionHandler.java — returns GenericResponse on errors
  - config/SwaggerConfig.java (or shared from repo root)
• application.properties — server.port=8101

Endpoints:
• GET /api/v1/employees?page=0&size=10&sort=lastName,asc — paginated list
• GET /api/v1/employees/{id}
• POST /api/v1/employees — @Valid on EmployeeRequest
• PUT /api/v1/employees/{id}
• DELETE /api/v1/employees/{id}
• GET /api/v1/employees/department/{dept}
• GET /api/v1/employees/search?keyword=john
• GET /actuator/health — health check

Key concepts: DTO pattern decouples API from database schema. Pageable for pagination. @Valid + @NotBlank/@Email on DTOs. Never expose entity directly — use EmployeeResponse.
gh-w10-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: Production APIs never return JPA entities — lazy-loading can trigger 500 errors, and internal fields leak. DTOs + pagination are table stakes for any backend role.

Run:
cd EmployeeAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Swagger UI: http://localhost:8101/swagger-ui.html

Push to springboot-week-3-rest-api:
git add EmployeeAPI/
git commit -m "Add EmployeeAPI — DTOs, pagination, Swagger"
git push

gh-w10-f2.answer:
HospitalAPI/ — Hospital Management REST API with nested resources, API versioning, and comprehensive documentation.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — web, data-jpa, mysql, validation, springdoc-openapi
• model/Patient.java, Doctor.java, Appointment.java
• dto/ for each entity + nested request/response DTOs
• controller/PatientController — /api/v1/patients
• controller/DoctorController — /api/v1/doctors
• controller/AppointmentController — nested routes
• config/SwaggerConfig.java

Endpoints (v1):
• CRUD /api/v1/patients
• CRUD /api/v1/doctors
• GET /api/v1/patients/{patientId}/appointments — nested resource
• POST /api/v1/patients/{patientId}/appointments — schedule for patient
• GET /api/v1/doctors/{doctorId}/appointments?date=2026-06-20
• GET /api/v1/appointments/{id}
• PUT /api/v1/appointments/{id}
• DELETE /api/v1/appointments/{id}

Versioning (bonus v2):
• GET /api/v2/appointments — returns extended response with doctor specialization included

Use GenericResponse wrapper for all responses. Health check at /actuator/health.

Key concepts: Nested URIs (/patients/1/appointments) express ownership. URI versioning (/api/v1 vs /api/v2) is the most common versioning strategy.
gh-w10-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: Hospital systems expose nested resources — a patient's appointments belong to that patient. API versioning lets you add fields without breaking existing mobile apps.

Run:
cd HospitalAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test nested:
POST /api/v1/patients/1/appointments
{"doctorId":2,"appointmentDateTime":"2026-06-25T10:00:00","notes":"Checkup"}

Push:
git add HospitalAPI/
git commit -m "Add HospitalAPI — nested resources and v1/v2 versioning"
git push

gh-w10-f3.answer:
ECommerceProductAPI/ — E-Commerce Product catalog API with search, filter, pagination, categories, and rate limiting.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — web, data-jpa, mysql, validation, springdoc-openapi, bucket4j or spring rate limiter
• model/Product.java — name, description, sku, price, categoryId, stock, imageUrl
• model/Category.java — id, name, description
• dto/ProductRequest, ProductResponse, CategoryResponse
• repository/ProductRepository — search with @Query
• service/ProductService, CategoryService
• controller/ProductController — /api/v1/products
• controller/CategoryController — /api/v1/categories
• config/RateLimitConfig.java — optional

Endpoints:
• GET /api/v1/products?page=0&size=20&sort=price,desc — paginated
• GET /api/v1/products/{id}
• POST /api/v1/products — create
• PUT /api/v1/products/{id}
• DELETE /api/v1/products/{id}
• GET /api/v1/products/search?keyword=laptop&category=electronics&minPrice=100&maxPrice=5000
• GET /api/v1/categories
• GET /api/v1/categories/{id}/products
• GET /api/v1/products/low-stock?threshold=10

Key concepts: Query params for filtering (not new endpoints per filter). Pagination mandatory for product lists. Rate limiting protects public catalog from abuse.
gh-w10-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: Amazon-style product search combines keyword, category, and price range in one endpoint. Pagination prevents loading 100k products into memory.

Run:
cd ECommerceProductAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test search:
GET /api/v1/products/search?keyword=phone&minPrice=5000&maxPrice=50000&page=0&size=10

Push:
git add ECommerceProductAPI/
git commit -m "Add ECommerceProductAPI — search, filter, pagination"
git push

gh-w10-f4.answer:
CRMAPI/ — Customer Relationship Management REST API with contacts, leads, activity tracking, and full documentation.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — web, data-jpa, mysql, validation, springdoc-openapi
• model/Contact.java — name, email, phone, company, status
• model/Lead.java — title, source, status (NEW/CONTACTED/QUALIFIED/CONVERTED/LOST), assignedTo, contactId
• model/Activity.java — type (CALL/EMAIL/MEETING), description, leadId, createdAt
• dto/ for each + LeadConversionRequest
• repository/ with custom queries
• controller/ContactController, LeadController, ActivityController

Endpoints:
• CRUD /api/v1/contacts
• CRUD /api/v1/leads
• GET /api/v1/leads?status=NEW&page=0&size=10 — filter + pagination
• POST /api/v1/leads/{id}/activities — log activity on lead
• GET /api/v1/leads/{id}/activities
• POST /api/v1/leads/{id}/convert — bonus: convert lead to contact/customer
• GET /api/v1/leads/search?keyword=enterprise

All responses wrapped in GenericResponse. OpenAPI annotations on every endpoint.

Key concepts: CRM tracks sales pipeline — leads move through statuses. Activity logs create audit trail. Lead conversion is a state transition, not just CRUD.
gh-w10-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: Salesforce, HubSpot, and every B2B startup build CRM APIs. Lead status filtering + activity logging mirrors real sales tooling.

Run:
cd CRMAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test lead flow:
1. POST /api/v1/leads — create lead (status NEW)
2. POST /api/v1/leads/1/activities — log CALL
3. PUT /api/v1/leads/1 — status QUALIFIED
4. POST /api/v1/leads/1/convert

Push:
git add CRMAPI/
git commit -m "Add CRMAPI — contacts, leads, activities"
git push

gh-w10-f5.answer:
StudentAPI/ — Student Management REST API with validation, idempotent POST, correlation ID logging, and production structure.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — web, data-jpa, mysql, validation, springdoc-openapi
• model/Student.java, Course.java, Enrollment.java
• dto/StudentRequest, StudentResponse, EnrollmentRequest
• filter/CorrelationIdFilter.java — reads/generates X-Correlation-Id header
• service/StudentService, EnrollmentService
• controller/StudentController — /api/v1/students
• controller/EnrollmentController — /api/v1/students/{id}/courses

Endpoints:
• CRUD /api/v1/students with @Valid
• POST /api/v1/students with Idempotency-Key header — bonus: same key returns same result without duplicate
• GET /api/v1/students/{id}/courses — nested enrollments
• POST /api/v1/students/{id}/courses — enroll in course
• DELETE /api/v1/students/{id}/courses/{courseId} — drop course

CorrelationIdFilter:
• Read X-Correlation-Id from request or generate UUID
• Put in MDC for logging: MDC.put("correlationId", id)
• Return same ID in response header

Key concepts: Idempotency prevents duplicate records on network retry. Correlation IDs trace one request across logs — essential for debugging production.
gh-w10-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: Payment and enrollment systems require idempotent POST — if the client retries, you don't create two records. Correlation IDs appear in every production log aggregation tool (ELK, Datadog).

Run:
cd StudentAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test correlation ID:
curl -H "X-Correlation-Id: abc-123" http://localhost:8105/api/v1/students

Check logs for correlationId=abc-123.

Push:
git add StudentAPI/
git commit -m "Add StudentAPI — validation, correlation ID, idempotency"
git push

gh-w10-f6.answer:
SwaggerConfig.java — OpenAPI/Swagger configuration shared across Week 10 projects.

Requirements:
1. @Configuration class with @OpenAPIDefinition for API metadata.
2. Info — title, version, description, contact (your name/email).
3. Server URL — http://localhost:8080 (or document multiple servers per project).
4. @Bean OpenAPI customOpenAPI() — configure components if needed.
5. Group APIs with tags matching controllers (@Tag on each controller).
6. Add springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui dependency in pom.xml.
7. Access Swagger UI at /swagger-ui.html after running any project.

Optional enhancements:
• Document common error responses (400, 404, 500)
• Add security scheme if JWT is used
• Custom OperationCustomizer for correlation ID header

Key concepts: OpenAPI is the machine-readable spec; Swagger UI is the human-friendly browser view. springdoc-openapi auto-generates spec from your annotations.
gh-w10-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: Swagger UI lets frontend teams explore and test APIs without Postman. OpenAPI specs can generate client SDKs automatically — standard in modern API teams.

Dependency (pom.xml):
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springdoc</groupId>
  <artifactId>springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui</artifactId>
  <version>2.5.0</version>
</dependency>

Run any project → open http://localhost:<port>/swagger-ui.html

Push:
git add SwaggerConfig.java
git commit -m "Add SwaggerConfig — OpenAPI documentation setup"
git push

gh-w10-f7.answer:
GenericResponse.java — standardized API response wrapper used across all Week 10 projects.

Requirements:
1. Generic class GenericResponse<T> with fields: success (boolean), message (String), data (T), timestamp (LocalDateTime), errors (List<String> optional).
2. Static factory methods:
   • success(T data) — success=true, message="OK", data populated
   • success(String message, T data)
   • error(String message) — success=false, data=null
   • error(String message, List<String> errors) — validation errors
3. Use for all controller responses — consistent JSON shape for frontend.
4. GlobalExceptionHandler returns GenericResponse on exceptions.

Example JSON:
Success: {"success":true,"message":"OK","data":{...},"timestamp":"2026-06-20T10:00:00"}
Error: {"success":false,"message":"Validation failed","errors":["email must be valid"],"timestamp":"..."}

Key concepts: Consistent response envelope lets frontend handle one shape. Separate business data (data) from metadata (success, message, timestamp).
gh-w10-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: Frontend apps check response.success before reading data — one pattern everywhere. Companies like Stripe and GitHub use similar wrapper patterns in their APIs.

Copy into each project's common/dto/ package or share as a module.

Push:
git add GenericResponse.java
git commit -m "Add GenericResponse — standardized API wrapper"
git push

gh-w10-f8.answer:
Postman Collection.json — comprehensive Postman collection with test scripts for all five Week 10 production APIs.

Requirements:
1. Collection: "Spring Boot Week 10 — Production REST APIs".
2. Folders per API: EmployeeAPI (8101), HospitalAPI (8102), ECommerceProductAPI (8103), CRMAPI (8104), StudentAPI (8105).
3. Variables for each baseUrl.
4. Every request includes Tests tab:
   • Status code check
   • Response time under 2000ms
   • JSON schema validation (response has success field for wrapped APIs)
5. Pagination tests — verify page, size, totalElements in response.
6. Error case tests — POST invalid body → expect 400.
7. Pre-request scripts for unique data (timestamp in email).
8. Collection-level description documenting API versioning (/api/v1).

Key concepts: Postman tests turn manual checking into repeatable regression suite. Newman runs collections in CI pipelines.
gh-w10-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: Before deploy, teams run Postman collections to verify no endpoint broke. Test scripts catch status code regressions that humans miss when skimming JSON.

Import and Run Collection after starting all five APIs.

Optional CI:
npm install -g newman
newman run "Postman Collection.json"

Push:
git add "Postman Collection.json"
git commit -m "Add Postman collection with automated tests"
git push

gh-w10-f9.answer:
openapi.yaml — OpenAPI 3.0 specification documenting all Week 10 API endpoints.

Requirements:
1. openapi: 3.0.3 at top.
2. info — title, version, description.
3. servers — list all five API base URLs with ports.
4. paths — document at minimum:
   • /api/v1/employees (GET with page/size/sort params, POST)
   • /api/v1/employees/{id} (GET, PUT, DELETE)
   • /api/v1/patients/{patientId}/appointments (GET, POST)
   • /api/v1/products/search (GET with query params)
   • /api/v1/leads (GET, POST)
   • /api/v1/students (GET, POST)
5. components/schemas — EmployeeRequest, EmployeeResponse, GenericResponse, ErrorResponse.
6. components/parameters — PageParam, SizeParam.
7. tags — Employees, Patients, Products, Leads, Students.

Can be auto-generated from springdoc (/v3/api-docs.yaml) then merged, or written manually for portfolio clarity.

Key concepts: OpenAPI is the contract. Frontend, mobile, and QA all reference this file. YAML is human-readable; JSON is equivalent.
gh-w10-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: OpenAPI specs import into Postman, Swagger Editor, and code generators. Checking openapi.yaml into Git is how API-first teams work — design the contract, then implement.

Validate:
npm install -g @apidevtools/swagger-cli
swagger-cli validate openapi.yaml

View: paste into https://editor.swagger.io

Push:
git add openapi.yaml
git commit -m "Add OpenAPI 3.0 spec for Week 10 APIs"
git push
gh-w10-f9.code:
openapi: 3.0.3
info:
  title: Prathyu Academy Production REST APIs
  version: 1.0.0
  description: Week 10 production-quality REST APIs

servers:
  - url: http://localhost:8101
    description: EmployeeAPI
  - url: http://localhost:8102
    description: HospitalAPI

tags:
  - name: Employees
    description: Employee management

paths:
  /api/v1/employees:
    get:
      tags: [Employees]
      summary: List employees with pagination
      parameters:
        - name: page
          in: query
          schema: { type: integer, default: 0 }
        - name: size
          in: query
          schema: { type: integer, default: 10 }
        - name: sort
          in: query
          schema: { type: string, default: "lastName,asc" }
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Paginated employee list
    post:
      tags: [Employees]
      summary: Create employee
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: '#/components/schemas/EmployeeRequest'
      responses:
        '201':
          description: Employee created

components:
  schemas:
    GenericResponse:
      type: object
      properties:
        success: { type: boolean }
        message: { type: string }
        data: { type: object }
        timestamp: { type: string, format: date-time }
    EmployeeRequest:
      type: object
      required: [firstName, lastName, email]
      properties:
        firstName: { type: string }
        lastName: { type: string }
        email: { type: string, format: email }
        department: { type: string }
        salary: { type: number }

gh-w10-f10.code:
# springboot-week-3-rest-api

Week 10 — Production REST API Engineering for Prathyu Academy.

## Production Features
- DTO pattern (never expose entities)
- Pagination & sorting (`?page=0&size=10&sort=name,asc`)
- Standardized `GenericResponse<T>` wrapper
- OpenAPI/Swagger documentation
- Input validation with `@Valid`
- Correlation ID tracing
- API versioning (`/api/v1/`)

## Projects
| Project | Port | Highlights |
|---------|------|------------|
| EmployeeAPI | 8101 | DTOs, pagination, Swagger |
| HospitalAPI | 8102 | Nested resources, v1/v2 |
| ECommerceProductAPI | 8103 | Search, filter, categories |
| CRMAPI | 8104 | Leads, activities, conversion |
| StudentAPI | 8105 | Idempotency, correlation ID |

## Quick Start
```bash
cd EmployeeAPI
mvn spring-boot:run
# Swagger UI: http://localhost:8101/swagger-ui.html
```

## Documentation
- `openapi.yaml` — OpenAPI 3.0 spec
- `Postman Collection.json` — test suite with scripts

## What I Learned
I built production-style REST APIs with consistent response envelopes, pagination, and OpenAPI documentation — patterns used in real backend teams.
